William is the link between the confirmed paternal line and the compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker. Research needed to trace descendants.

military_service.unit: Capt. Caleb Bull's Company in Webb's Regiment, Continental Army
military_service.notes: Enlisted Apr 21, 1777 as Private for 3 years in Capt. Caleb Bull's Company, Webb's Regiment. Promoted Sergeant May 16, 1780 for an additional 8 months. Member of the Hartford 'band of music' (eight musicians) raised 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line. Pensioned Nov 4, 1818. DAR Ancestor #A057709.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford band of music attached to Connecticut line
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was a member of the eight-man 'band of music' raised in Hartford in 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line of the Continental Army — one of the rare documented Revolutionary War regimental musical ensembles. The band provided drums, fifes, and field music during the war's middle years. The arrangement was unusual: most Revolutionary regiments had ad hoc field musicians rather than a formally organized eight-piece band.

narrative_blocks.header: Three years Private, eight months Sergeant 1777-1780
narrative_blocks.body: William enlisted April 21, 1777 as a Private in Captain Caleb Bull's Company of Webb's Regiment for a three-year term. On May 16, 1780 he was promoted to Sergeant for an additional eight months of service. He was pensioned November 4, 1818 (DAR Ancestor #A057709) — the federal pension secured nearly four decades after his service ended.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children with Sarah Wilcox of Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: William married Sarah Wilcox of Berlin Connecticut and they had nine documented children together. The family remained in Kensington, the original Berlin Connecticut parish where William had been born in 1756 and where he would die in 1826. The nine-child household was characteristic of the post-Revolutionary Connecticut farming generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Kensington Berlin CT 1756 to 1826 age sixty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was born September 15, 1756 in Kensington (now Berlin) Connecticut and died February 4, 1826 in Kensington at age sixty-nine. He is buried at West Lane Cemetery in Berlin Connecticut. His Revolutionary War service spanned the war's middle period from the bleakest 1777 Valley Forge winter through the war's final 1780-1781 phase.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

documents.document_blurb: 1838 letter from former Dover neighbor Mott Titus describing his Ohio migration, the canal boom, frontier economics, and the Whig sweep of New York's 1838 elections.

career.title: Physician
career.notes: Practiced medicine in South Dover Dutchess County NY for decades.

career.title: State Legislator
career.notes: Represented the town of Dover in the NY State Legislature; described as a prominent citizen.

narrative_blocks.header: South Dover physician served NY legislature
narrative_blocks.body: William left Berlin Connecticut for Dutchess County New York where he settled as a physician at South Dover. He became a prominent citizen and represented the town of Dover in the New York State Legislature. He died at Dover Furnace in March 1853 at age sixty-five.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia carried Hooker blood from both parents — her mother Ann Hooker Stanley descended from Samuel Hooker Jr., her father Seth Stanley descended through his mother Hannah Hart from Abigail Hooker. When she married her cousin Dr. William Hooker in Hartford on January 9, 1814, three Hooker branches converged in their two children — a triple descent that closed a century-and-a-half family loop.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Raised five children at his cousin-married parents' homestead
narrative_blocks.body: William Jr. inherited and raised his five children at the Dover Furnace homestead his physician father had established after leaving Berlin Connecticut. He and Sarah Varney Cutler stayed in Dover their entire married life. He is buried at South Dover Rural Cemetery beside both of his cousin-married parents.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: He died at New Haven; she at Newark
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Berlin, Connecticut March 17, 1799, fourth child of William Hooker and Hannah Jones. He attended Yale and practiced medicine, marrying Eliza Beers — herself born 1799 — at New Haven on April 3, 1823. She died at Newark, New Jersey in 1862; he followed at New Haven on March 19, 1863, aged sixty-four, and was buried at Evergreen Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and married two years apart
narrative_blocks.body: He and Eliza Beers were born the same year, 1799, married at New Haven in 1823, and died within a year of each other — she in 1862, he in 1863. The Berlin-born physician raised at least nine children before his death at New Haven.

burial.plot_notes: Evergreen Cemetery, New Haven, New Haven County CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband Albert Baldwin by four years
narrative_blocks.body: Jennette Phelps Hooker married Albert Baldwin of Newark, New Jersey, and they had three children in Newark before Albert died in 1879. Jennette survived him by four years, dying in 1883 at forty-six — leaving her eldest son Charles an orphan at seventeen.
Levi died in 1808 at the age of six.

primary_residence.notes: Seth Hooker of Kensington — hatter and farmer on the Gay place at Hinsdale NH

bio.notes: FVR spells name 'Marah Burnham'; EH gives 'Sarah Burnham'

cross_connections.display_label: His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably
